intro_script: Welcome back! It's June 13th, 2026, and your daily dose of robotics news is here. Today we cover urban autonomy advancements, autonomous freight in action, and what’s next for humanoid design across eight big stories.

items.title: QCraft demonstrates urban NOA on Qualcomm’s latest Snapdragon Ride chip, targets 2026 global mass production
items.summary: QCraft showcased its urban Navigate-on-Autopilot (NOA) system utilizing Qualcomm's SA8650P Snapdragon Ride chip. The demonstration occurred at the Automotive Technology and Cooperation Summit in Wuxi, China on June 5th. During the event, attendees experienced live test rides featuring the technology in production vehicles. This achievement positions QCraft for global mass production of its autonomous driving solutions.
items.audio_script: From Robotics & Automation News, "QCraft demonstrates urban NOA on Qualcomm’s latest Snapdragon Ride chip, targets 2026 global mass production." This report details QCraft's advancement of its self-driving system using the newest processor from Qualcomm and outlines upcoming production goals. The demonstration took place at the Automotive Technology and Cooperation Summit held in Wuxi, China, on June fifth. During this event, live test rides showcased the urban Navigate-on-Autopilot capability integrated into operational vehicles. This successful display positions QCraft to move toward global mass production of its autonomous driving hardware and software solutions. Industry analysts anticipate this announcement signals a significant step toward wider commercial deployment of advanced driver assistance technologies in the near term.

items.title: Gatik to bring autonomous freight to PepsiCo’s North American supply chain
items.summary: Gatik will integrate autonomous freight technology into PepsiCo's operational infrastructure. This partnership specifically targets the company’s regional transportation networks across North America. Autonomous vehicles will handle the daily movement of products between various sites within the supply chain. Coverage of this development was initially reported by The Robot Report. The collaboration aims to enhance logistical efficiency for PepsiCo's product distribution.
items.audio_script: From The Robot Report, "Gatik to bring autonomous freight to PepsiCo’s North American supply chain." This development centers on the integration of self-driving vehicle technology into PepsiCo's established operational framework. The partnership is designed to specifically enhance regional transportation networks across the continent. Autonomous vehicles will undertake the regular transit of goods between multiple points within the corporate supply infrastructure, thereby aiming for improved logistical efficiency in product distribution. Industry observers note this represents a significant deployment example of autonomous commercial trucking in large-scale consumer goods logistics operations going forward.

items.title: Robotics Summit panel explores the state of humanoid robot design
items.summary: Experts at the Robotics Summit & Expo discussed the current state of humanoid robotics. A key area of focus during the panel was cost reduction. Attendees explored pathways to achieving a low unit price for these robots, specifically targeting a \$20,000 point. Coverage detailing this discussion appeared on The Robot Report.
items.audio_script: From The Robot Report, "Robotics Summit panel explores the state of humanoid robot design." The discussion centered on the current technological landscape and the economic feasibility of advanced humanoid robots. Experts present at the summit dedicated significant time to analyzing cost-reduction strategies for these complex machines. Several pathways were reviewed, with a particular emphasis placed on achieving a low unit price point, specifically identifying \$20,000 as an attainable benchmark through streamlined manufacturing and component sourcing improvements. This focus suggests a tangible industry movement toward broader commercial deployment timelines.

items.title: MassRobotics announces the winners of 2026 Robotics Medal and Rising Star awards
items.summary: Allison Okamura and Ayoung Kim were named the recipients of MassRobotics' 2026 Robotics Medal and Rising Star Medal. These accolades specifically recognize achievements by women within the field of robotics. The announcement detailing these winners was published on The Robot Report.
items.audio_script: From The Robot Report, "MassRobotics announces the winners of 2026 Robotics Medal and Rising Star awards." This announcement details the recipients of significant accolades designed to recognize notable contributions by women within the field of robotics. Both Allison Okamura and Ayoung Kim were named as the awardees for their respective honors. The ceremony was established to specifically highlight achievements made by women in the complex domain of robotic development and engineering practice. Following rigorous evaluation, the awards confirm these two individuals are the recognized leaders for 2026 across both categories. This recognition underscores the increasing prominence and vital contributions of female talent throughout the global artificial intelligence and technology sectors.

items.title: Video Friday: Robotic Motion Discovery Reveals Unusual Behaviors
items.summary: MotionDisco is a framework that generates contact-rich, long-horizon humanoid motions without relying on human demonstrations. Other featured videos demonstrate advanced applications, including an HSR assisting residents in real homes and a bipedal robot performing aerial braking maneuvers. MIT's work involved developing a robotic shoulder-friend for computer human interaction research. For manipulation, the fully open-source MIDAS Hand platform is presented, while ROBOTIS showcases using video capture via smartphone cameras to generate humanoid motions for AI Sapiens. Finally, the article highlights various collaborations and projects pushing the boundaries of robotics, from space inspection swarms to home assistance.
items.audio_script:
From IEEE Spectrum - Robotics, "Video Friday: Robotic Motion Discovery Reveals Unusual Behaviors." This report details several recent advancements in robotic motion generation and application across various functional domains. The coverage includes the introduction of MotionDisco, a framework capable of creating contact-rich, long-horizon humanoid motions without requiring human demonstrations for training. Featured examples demonstrate an HSR system assisting residents in actual homes and a bipedal robot executing aerial braking maneuvers. Further technology presented involved MIT's development of a robotic shoulder designed for computer human interaction research, alongside the open-source MIDAS Hand platform for manipulation tasks.

Other showcased techniques included ROBOTIS utilizing video capture from standard smartphone cameras to generate humanoid motions for AI Sapiens. The article rounded out its coverage by highlighting diverse collaborations advancing robotics, such as space inspection swarms and enhanced domestic assistance systems. These developments signal a growing trajectory toward increasingly capable and versatile autonomous robotic interaction with complex real-world environments.

items.title: ROS Discourse General: ROS2 multi-machine with WSL2 + Linux (NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano)
items.summary: A user is attempting to establish ROS2 multi-machine communication between a Windows 11 host running WSL2 (Ubuntu 22.04) and an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano. The goal is for the WSL2 machine to generate data that the native Linux platform can process. Despite connecting both machines to the same network and configuring WSL2 networking in mirrored mode, the user cannot achieve topic or node visibility between the two systems. Attempts using Cyclone and FastDDS with custom configurations have been unsuccessful. The author is requesting guidance on successfully setting up ROS2 communication across these specific hardware/OS boundaries.
items.audio_script: From Planet ROS, "ROS Discourse General: ROS2 multi-machine with WSL2 + Linux (NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano)". The discussion details a technical challenge involving the establishment of bidirectional communication for ROS2 across disparate operating systems and hardware platforms. A user reported difficulties achieving topic or node visibility between a Windows 11 host running within WSL2 on Ubuntu 22.04, and an attached NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano running native Linux. Despite connecting both machines to a shared network and configuring the WSL2 environment in mirrored mode, standard networking approaches failed to facilitate communication, even when utilizing specialized middleware like Cyclone or FastDDS with custom parameter settings. Resolving this cross-boundary connectivity issue remains a key focus for developers integrating embedded AI systems with desktop development environments.

items.title: ROS Discourse General: STS3215 pan/tilt + LD19: a no-SDK 3D scanning module for ROS 2 Jazzy
items.summary: A fully functional ROS 2 subsystem converts a pan/tilt platform and a 2D LiDAR into a no-SDK 3D scanner. This setup uses Feetech STS3215 servos to aim an LDROBOT LD19, with a node assembling 2D scans into `PointCloud2` using the live TF tree. The system is designed to function purely in ROS 2 or optionally communicate via MQTT for external control. It addresses technical hurdles like fixing an upstream LiDAR driver build issue and bypassing vendor SDKs by implementing serial protocols directly. This entire project is open-sourced under GPL-3.0 and works on platforms like the RK3588 and Pi 5.
items.audio_script: From Planet ROS, "ROS Discourse General: STS3215 pan/tilt + LD19: a no-SDK 3D scanning module for ROS 2 Jazzy." This report details the creation of a fully functional three-dimensional scanning subsystem utilizing readily available hardware components within the ROS 2 framework. The system integrates Feetech STS3215 servos to control an LDROBOT LD19, thereby transforming it into a scanner capable of generating point clouds. A dedicated node manages this process by assembling continuous two-dimensional scans into the standard PointCloud2 format, leveraging the live Transformation tree for spatial accuracy. Furthermore, the implementation successfully bypassed proprietary vendor SDKs through direct serial protocol integration and addresses upstream driver build complexities. The project is fully open-sourced under GPL-3.0 and operates on platforms such as the RK3588 and Pi 5. This development demonstrates a viable pathway for rapid, accessible deployment of sophisticated sensing capabilities in robotics research environments.

items.title: The Role of RF Connectors in Robotic Vision, Sensor Communication, and Automated Inspection Systems
items.summary: RF connectors play a critical, often overlooked role in robotic vision and automated inspection systems. These demanding applications rely on reliable physical signal pathways for high performance. Poor connectivity, such as dirty or vibrating interfaces, can degrade signals regardless of advanced software or powerful processing units. Camera data transmission and sensor communication are particularly vulnerable to these physical disruptions. Consequently, the robustness of RF connectors is paramount for consistent system operation in real-world environments.
items.audio_script: From Robotics & Automation News, "The Role of RF Connectors in Robotic Vision, Sensor Communication, and Automated Inspection Systems." This report details the critical, often underestimated function of radio frequency connectors within advanced robotic vision and automated inspection platforms. The analysis found that system performance in these demanding applications is highly dependent on the physical integrity of signal pathways. Specific attention was paid to camera data transmission and sensor communication, which proved susceptible to degradation caused by common physical issues like dirt accumulation or operational vibration at the interface level. Consequently, achieving consistent real-world operation necessitates robust RF connector designs capable of maintaining reliable electrical contact. These findings emphasize that hardware interconnectivity remains a foundational element for dependable autonomous system deployment across various industries.
